title: Guidelines in the management of orofacial pain/TMD: An evidence-based approach
research_question: What are the evidence-based guidelines for the treatment of orofacial pain and temporomandibular disorders (TMD) in dental practice?
methods_summary: Systematic literature search in Medline/PubMed, Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, DARE, HTA Database, and Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (1965–2014).
Pair-wise audit of publications by 12 specialists using GRADE methodology.
Expert panel ranking of condition severity and treatment priority (1–10 scale).
dataset: 218 studies (1965–2014) plus 63 additional studies (2008–2014) from systematic reviews, RCTs, clinical trials, and observational studies.
key_findings: Counseling and behavioral approaches are first-line interventions for TMD.
Stabilization appliances and jaw exercises have evidence of effect and cost-effectiveness, and may be combined with NSAIDs for limited periods.
Occlusal adjustment is contraindicated in acute TMJ arthralgia, acute arthritis, and dysocclusion (phantom bite).
Low-level laser and TENS have low or no evidence for TMD treatment.
Surgical interventions (arthroscopy, discectomy, arthrocentesis) are reserved for patients refractory to conservative treatment for at least 6 months.
extracted_insights: The Swedish national guidelines provide a comprehensive, evidence-based priority ranking for TMD treatments, integrating clinical effectiveness, health economics, and condition severity. Key recommendations emphasize reversible, conservative treatments (behavioral therapy, stabilization splints, jaw exercises) as first-line, while irreversible procedures (occlusal adjustment, surgery) are reserved for specific, refractory cases. The guidelines highlight the importance of biopsychosocial assessment due to common comorbidities with widespread pain and depression.
